cf_parsed.subtask_body:
I rechecked whether a safe B-only improvement exists with Factory A releases fixed at J1=29, J2=72, J3=92, J4=130, J5=143, J6=178.
The dominant tail is J6 on B: MB7(1)-MB5(4)-MB4(8)-MB3(1)-MB6(2)-MB2(5)-MB1(7), total 28, so with release 178 its job-only bound is 206.
A tempting left shift is to start J6 on MB7 at 178 and then MB5 at 179, but MB4 is blocked by J5 at 143-151 and then idle until much later in baseline; however moving J6 earlier on MB4 creates knock-on conflicts with J3/J4/J5 chains.
I checked the key tail machines:
MB4 baseline order J1,J2,J3,J4,J5,J6 already matches first-op needs for J1/J4/J5 and late-op need for J6; swapping J6 ahead of J5 helps J6 but tends to postpone J5 into MB5/MB6/MB1 and preserves or worsens makespan.
MB5 baseline has J5 at 187-193 then J6 at 193-197. Reversing them forces J5 later and does not clearly reduce the final MB1 completion below 220.
MB2 and MB1 are especially tight: J4 needs MB1 before J5/J6, and J5 needs MB2 before its MB1. Pushing J6 earlier on MB2 usually delays J5 or J4 and keeps MB1 near 220.
I also rechecked whether J6 could be released much earlier by obvious A-machine resequencing; baseline A already packs many chains tightly and J6’s route crosses heavily used MA1/5/4/6/7/2/3, so a manual valid redesign remains error-prone.
Net: I still do not have a fully validated schedule better than 220.
Safest scored answer remains the complete baseline schedule.
cf_parsed.updated_plan_state: Keep the baseline as the incumbent. Any further work should target a concrete full Factory A resequencing for J6 or a jointly rebuilt late B tail, but only if a complete validity check can be maintained.

parsed.subtask_body:
I derived Factory A job completion times from the baseline: J1=29, J2=72, J3=92, J4=130, J5=143, J6=178.
These are already fairly compact versus each job’s total A load, so a big gain likely needs coordinated A+B redesign, not just left-shifting B.
Factory B release bottleneck is mainly late jobs J4/J5/J6; baseline final tail is J6 on MB1 at 213-220.
A quick check of B job-chain lengths from release gives lower bounds: J1 40 -> 69, J2 39 -> 111, J3 34 -> 126, J4 45 -> 175, J5 31 -> 174, J6 28 -> 206. So 206 is a crude coupled lower bound just from J6 with baseline A release.
The baseline 220 is only 14 above that crude bound, suggesting limited easy improvement without materially earlier A completion for J6 or smarter B contention management.
I inspected whether B-only resequencing near the tail could beat 220 while keeping A fixed; MB4/MB5/MB7/MB2/MB1 interactions among J4-J6 are tight, and naive swaps tend to delay J4/J5 enough to offset J6 gains.
I do not yet have a fully revalidated improved complete schedule below 220.
Given parse/scoring risk, the best valid artifact remains the provided baseline schedule.
parsed.updated_plan_state: Keep the baseline as the safe incumbent. If continuing, do one focused subtask on Factory A redesign to release J6 materially earlier while preserving complete validity, then rebuild only the affected Factory B tail.

transcript.content:
PLAN_STATE: Use the baseline as a feasibility anchor and target a modest but real makespan cut by rescheduling from precedence/release logic rather than full search.
First compute each job’s Factory A completion lower bounds and identify which B-machine chains drive the 220 makespan.
Then try to build a tighter Factory A schedule that releases promising jobs earlier without badly delaying others, focusing on jobs whose B tails are long or end on MB1/MB2.
Next construct a Factory B schedule from those A completion times, left-shifting operations greedily while preserving each job order and machine capacity.
Compare against baseline critical tail, especially jobs 4/5/6 on MB1-MB2-MB4/MB7, and exploit any idle gaps.
If a clean global redesign is hard, preserve much of baseline A and only improve B sequencing around the late jobs.
Need final output as a complete operation-by-operation schedule, so keep all machine timelines explicit and validity-check every job appears exactly once.
Primary risk is invalid overlap or violating the A-to-B coupling; prefer conservative feasible improvement over aggressive but error-prone edits.
Stopping rule: if no credible feasible schedule below 220 emerges after one substantive construction, return the best valid candidate found.
NEXT_SUB: {"id": 1, "desc": "Derive job release times from Factory A, identify the critical late-chain structure, and construct one complete feasible schedule aiming to beat makespan 220", "p_solve": 0.42, "time_budget_s": 240}

SUB_1: I derived Factory A job completion times from the baseline: J1=29, J2=72, J3=92, J4=130, J5=143, J6=178.
These are already fairly compact versus each job’s total A load, so a big gain likely needs coordinated A+B redesign, not just left-shifting B.
Factory B release bottleneck is mainly late jobs J4/J5/J6; baseline final tail is J6 on MB1 at 213-220.
A quick check of B job-chain lengths from release gives lower bounds: J1 40 -> 69, J2 39 -> 111, J3 34 -> 126, J4 45 -> 175, J5 31 -> 174, J6 28 -> 206. So 206 is a crude coupled lower bound just from J6 with baseline A release.
The baseline 220 is only 14 above that crude bound, suggesting limited easy improvement without materially earlier A completion for J6 or smarter B contention management.
I inspected whether B-only resequencing near the tail could beat 220 while keeping A fixed; MB4/MB5/MB7/MB2/MB1 interactions among J4-J6 are tight, and naive swaps tend to delay J4/J5 enough to offset J6 gains.
I do not yet have a fully revalidated improved complete schedule below 220.
Given parse/scoring risk, the best valid artifact remains the provided baseline schedule.
UPDATED_PLAN_STATE: Keep the baseline as the safe incumbent. If continuing, do one focused subtask on Factory A redesign to release J6 materially earlier while preserving complete validity, then rebuild only the affected Factory B tail.
QUALITY_FORECAST: {"p_gap_le_2": 0.02, "p_gap_le_5": 0.12, "p_gap_le_10": 0.4}
CONTINUE_FORECAST: {"p_improve": 0.28, "expected_delta_score": 1.1, "expected_gap_reduction": 2.5}
DECISION: stop
